intro.value: A short reference sheet for algebraic and distribution identities that show up repeatedly in derivations.
These identities are written in a general form, so they can be reused outside the Kuramoto walkthroughs.

content.value: The Dirac delta does not spread weight over a neighborhood. It samples the rest of the integrand at the point where its argument vanishes.
If the selected point lies outside a finite integration interval, the whole integral is zero. That support condition is often what later turns into an indicator or new integration bounds.

reference.value: Use this form when a derivation says a delta 'collapses' an integral.

content.value: Euler's identity rewrites a complex phase factor into real and imaginary parts. This is usually the step that makes symmetry arguments visible.
On a symmetric interval, the imaginary sine term often drops out when it multiplies an even weight, leaving only the cosine projection.
After a change of variables, the integration bounds must be rewritten in the new variable. Old x-bounds do not stay attached to du.
If u = h(x) is one-to-one on the interval, map the endpoints directly: the new lower bound is h(a) and the new upper bound is h(b).
If the substitution is not one-to-one on the interval, split the integral into monotone pieces first. Otherwise one pair of new bounds does not describe the whole map correctly.

reference.latex: \int_a^b f(x)\,dx = \int_{h(a)}^{h(b)} f(x(u))\,\frac{dx}{du}\,du \quad \text{if } h \text{ is one-to-one on } [a,b]

reference.value: Equivalent viewpoint: solve the old support condition in terms of the remaining variable. That is why theta in a finite interval can turn into omega in minus K r to K r after the theta integral is collapsed.

content.value: These are the basic endpoint values that usually determine new bounds after a sine or cosine substitution.
On the locked branch from minus pi over 2 to pi over 2, sine reaches minus 1 and plus 1 at the endpoints, while cosine is 0 at the endpoints and 1 at theta equals 0.
When a derivation leaves a cosine-squared weight, the standard move is to rewrite it with the double-angle identity and then integrate term by term.
This is exactly the identity used in the onset step from Eq. 12 to the critical coupling formula.
When the numerator and denominator share the same inner expression, treat that repeated piece like a temporary variable and rewrite the integrand into simpler parts.
A common move is to assume a form like A over a plus b u plus B, then solve for A and B by matching coefficients. This is the same general idea as partial fractions: rewrite first, integrate second.
The goal is usually to reduce the integrand to one standard integral plus one easy constant term.

reference.latex: \text{match coefficients to solve for } A \text{ and } B

content.value: This is a common definite-integral result, not a basic trig identity. It usually comes from the substitution t equals tangent theta, which turns the denominator into a quadratic in t.
It is the standard form used in the Lorentzian evaluation from Eq. 13 to Eq. 14 after the integrand has been rewritten.
These definitions are what make symmetry cancellations precise. They show up whenever an integral is taken over a symmetric interval from minus a to plus a.
An odd integrand cancels between x and minus x, while an even integrand reinforces itself on both sides.
